votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements primality testing, matching the Coq specification for IsPrime.
The Python code correctly implements a standard primality test which matches the Coq specification for prime numbers.
The Python code correctly implements the primality test, matching the Coq specification for IsPrime.

The Python is_prime function correctly implements primality testing using trial division with 6k±1 optimization. The Coq spec defines IsPrime as n > 1 and all divisors being 1 or n, which is the standard definition of primality. The Python code returns True iff n is prime by this definition. The spec problem_31_spec states IsPrime(n) <-> output=true, which the Python translates as is_prime(n) == output, producing the same concrete boolean judgments for all inputs.

The Python is_prime function correctly implements primality testing: returns False for n<=1, True for primes, False for composites. The Coq spec IsPrime defines a prime as n>1 where all divisors are 1 or n. The Python problem_31_spec checks is_prime(n) == output, which matches the Coq biconditional IsPrime n <-> output = true. For all concrete inputs, both will agree on whether n is prime and whether output correctly reflects that.

Python implements a correct primality test for n>1 and returns False for n<=1, matching Coq’s IsPrime definition on all natural numbers; the spec equates output with is_prime’s result, aligning with IsPrime <-> output=true.
Python is_prime returns True exactly for n > 1 with no divisors other than 1 and n, matching the Coq IsPrime definition and the spec on all natural numbers; preconditions are unconstrained in both.
Python implements the standard primality test and returns True iff n is a prime natural number (>1), matching the Coq IsPrime definition; precondition is unconstrained in both.
